tasks.prompt: I want to get conversational in Spanish in 6 months. How should I actually learn it so it sticks?

checks.desc: States the skill as a concrete doable outcome with measurable success criteria
Uses active recall / self-testing rather than rereading
Sets a spaced review schedule with expanding intervals
Deliberate practice at the edge of ability with feedback
Feynman/teach-back or encode as evergreen notes in own words

tasks.golden: First make the goal concrete and measurable: not 'know Spanish' but 'able to hold a 10-minute unscripted conversation about daily life with a native speaker.' That's your test of done. Decompose it into sub-skills — listening, core vocabulary, speaking fluency, grammar patterns — and attack the bottleneck first, usually speaking and listening. Practice at the edge of ability: have real conversations where you fail some of the time and get immediate feedback or correction from a tutor or language partner. For vocabulary, use active recall — test yourself instead of rereading lists — and put words into an SRS with a spaced schedule at expanding intervals (1d, 3d, 7d, 21d) so a little forgetting strengthens the memory. Interleave topics rather than blocking one. Use the Feynman approach: try to explain a grammar rule in your own words and notice where you stall — that gap is your next lesson. Finally, encode what sticks as short evergreen notes in your own words. This takes daily struggle, but in six months it will stick.
tasks.decoy: Honestly, just listen passively to Spanish podcasts in the background and let it soak in effortlessly. Highlight and reread your textbook a lot, and cram the night before any conversation. Six months is plenty of time without much work.
